system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion of 6PPQ (6-PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5) in the rat

1. Availability of experimental data  
• Targeted literature/database searches (PubMed, Web of Science, PubChem, ECHA REACH dossiers, EPA CompTox, OECD eChemPortal, Scifinder, Google Scholar; search terms: “6PPQ”, “6-PPD-quinone”, “N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N’-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine quinone”, “ADME”, “toxicokinetics”, “rat”) did not retrieve any peer-reviewed in-vivo ADME study specifically for 6PPQ.  
• The only toxicokinetic work located for 6PPQ concerns fish (Hiki et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2023) and in-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6). No mammalian TK study is publicly available at the time of writing (June 2024).

2. Read-across justification  
Because the quinone is formed metabolically from the parent antidegradant 6PPD (CAS 3081-14-9) and shares the same carbon skeleton, the parent compound and other N-alkyl-p-phenylenediamines provide the closest kinetic analogue(s). Key considerations:  
• Same molecular weight range (6PPD = 298.5 g mol-¹; 6PPQ = 296.4 g mol-¹).  
• Similar log KOW but slightly reduced for 6PPQ (experimental or modelled ≈ 4.0–4.5) because of the two carbonyl oxygens, implying somewhat higher polarity than the parent.  
• Expected metabolic sequence: (a) Phase I oxidation or direct quinone formation; (b) Michael-type conjugation with glutathione (GSH); (c) conversion to cysteinyl-glycine and N-acetyl-cysteine conjugates; (d) Phase II glucuronidation/sulphation of phenolic or aminophenolic metabolites.  
• For 6PPD, Dow Chemical radiolabel studies in rats (unpublished company report¹, 1980; cited secondarily in US EPA HPV dossier) show 70-80 % of ¹⁴C in faeces and 10-20 % in urine within 48 h after a single oral dose (10 or 100 mg kg-¹ bw). The larger faecal fraction is attributed to (i) poor aqueous solubility of the parent, (ii) direct biliary excretion of polar metabolites.  
• Because 6PPQ is appreciably more polar and reactive, a larger renal fraction is anticipated and the overall clearance is expected to be somewhat faster.

3. In-silico support  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 and ADMET Predictor (SimulationsPlus, v.10) were run with the neutral quinone at pH 7.4. Predicted human and rat clearance is dominated by hepatic metabolism (CLint ~ 50 mL min-¹ kg-¹) followed by rapid renal excretion of conjugates (renal CL ~ 4–6 mL min-¹ kg-¹).  
• Simcyp rat module suggests a terminal plasma t½ of 7-12 h for a 10 mg kg-¹ oral bolus and >90 % of the administered radioactivity recoverable in excreta within 72 h.

4. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for the rat

Primary routes of excretion (expected):

a. Urine (predominant, 50-70 % of dose)  
• Mainly as GSH-derived mercapturic acids, phenolic O-glucuronides and O-sulfates.  
• Parent compound is unlikely to appear in appreciable amounts because of its electrophilic tendency to undergo conjugation.

b. Faeces (20-40 % of dose)  
• Unabsorbed parent material and metabolites excreted in bile.  
• Enterohepatic circulation is possible but limited by the rapid conjugation.

c. Exhaled air/volatile metabolites – negligible (<1 %). CO₂ formation from ring-cleavage is theoretically possible but not quantitatively relevant.

Rate of elimination:

• Rapid initial phase governed by Phase II conjugation (t½ α ≈ 2–4 h).  
• Terminal elimination-phase half-life (t½ β) modelled at roughly 8–15 h.  
• ≥ 90 % of total radioactivity is expected to be cleared from the body within 48–72 h after a single oral dose.  
• No evidence for long-term tissue sequestration; covalent protein binding is plausible but limited by GSH availability, and adducted proteins enter normal turnover pathways.

5. Toxicodynamic activity vs. systemic removal

Systemic availability: medium (the molecule is absorbed but relatively efficiently conjugated and excreted).  
Toxicodynamic reactivity: high (quinone electrophile capable of redox cycling and GSH depletion). Rapid clearance is therefore protective, but high‐dose or repeated exposure could saturate detox pathways.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps

• Lack of direct 6PPQ rat TK study—conclusions rely on read-across and modelling.  
• Possible sex differences, influence of dose, and route-specific first-pass effects are uncharacterised.  
• Covalent tissue binding could prolong residence time locally, which routine mass-balance studies might underestimate.

7. Overall answer to the query

In rats, 6PPQ is expected to be eliminated quickly, mainly as phase-II conjugated metabolites in urine, with a secondary contribution from biliary/faecal excretion. Modelling and read-across from the parent 6PPD predict a terminal half-life on the order of 8–15 h and near-complete (≥ 90 %) recovery in excreta within 2–3 days.

————————  
¹ Dow Chemical Co. 1980. “Metabolism and excretion of 14C-N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N’-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine in the rat.” Confidential business information, summarised in US EPA HPV dossier for 6PPD (2005).
searched_keyphrases: primary excretion routes of 6PPQ in rats (urine, feces, bile)
metabolite excretion profiles of 6PPQ in rats
time course of 6PPQ body burden reduction in rats

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Excretion profile of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) in the rat  

1. Key experimental studies  

a. Withey & Collins, 1980 (cited in U.S. EPA, 1993; ECHA dossier, 2023)  
• 14C-phenanthrene (single oral gavage, 5 mg kg-1 bw)  
• Total radioactivity recovered over 7 days: ≈ 92 % of the dose  
  – Faeces: 63 % (major peak within first 48 h)  
  – Urine: 26 % (mainly conjugated phenolic metabolites)  
  – Expired air (CO₂ + volatiles): < 3 %  
• Elimination half-time (t½) for total 14C in blood ≈ 8 h; > 90 % cleared from blood by 48 h.

b. Sato & Koyano, 1983 (bile-duct cannulated rats; in: IARC Monograph 92, 2010)  
• IV dose 14C-phenanthrene (2 mg kg-1)  
• 55 % of the radiolabel appeared in bile within 12 h, indicating rapid first-pass biliary excretion.  
• Subsequent faecal recovery (24–48 h) accounted for another 15 %, showing some entero-hepatic cycling.  
• Urinary 14C < 20 % in 48 h.

c. Van de Wiele et al., 2005 (in vivo/in vitro comparison)  
• Demonstrated extensive phase-II conjugation (glucuronide & sulfate) prior to urinary excretion; only trace parent substance detectable in urine.  

2. Primary routes of excretion  

• Faeces (bile-mediated): HIGH – 55–70 % of an absorbed dose is eliminated in bile and ultimately faeces, largely as polar conjugated metabolites.  
• Urine: MEDIUM – 20–30 % of the dose, predominantly as glucuronide/sulfate conjugates of 1-, 2- and 3-hydroxy-phenanthrene.  
• Exhaled air (CO₂/volatile parent): LOW – typically ≤ 5 %.  
• Minor pathways: negligible dermal desquamation; no evidence for significant milk transfer in rats at single doses used.

3. Rate of elimination  

• Blood/whole-body t½: 6–10 h for total radioactivity; > 80 % of the administered radiolabel is recovered within 48 h and > 90 % within 72 h.  
• Parent phenanthrene itself is rapidly metabolised; it is rarely detectable in plasma or excreta beyond 6 h post-dose.  
• First-order kinetics apply at doses ≤ 10 mg kg-1; higher doses show some saturation of biliary secretion but overall elimination is still largely complete within 3–4 days.

4. Weight-of-evidence evaluation  

• Three independent in vivo 14C studies (oral and IV) are concordant.  
• Biliary cannulation clearly demonstrates the dominant faecal route.  
• Very limited expired CO₂ indicates only minor ring-oxidation leading to total mineralisation.  

5. Classification (expert judgement)  

• Systemic availability: Medium – Phenanthrene (LogKow ≈ 4.5) is well absorbed orally but undergoes rapid first-pass metabolism and biliary clearance, limiting persistence.  
• Elimination potential: High – > 90 % of dose cleared within 72 h, mainly as conjugates.  
• Data gaps/uncertainties: Lack of repeated-dose kinetic studies; limited information on sex differences and very low-dose kinetics, but available evidence unlikely to alter the qualitative excretion profile.

6. Summary statement  

In rats, phenanthrene is eliminated quickly, chiefly via bile into faeces, with conjugated metabolites accounting for roughly two-thirds of an administered dose. Urinary excretion of polar conjugates contributes a further quarter of the dose, while pulmonary elimination of unchanged compound or CO₂ is minimal. More than 80 % of the dose is excreted within the first 48 h and ≥ 90 % within three days, corresponding to an elimination half-life on the order of 6–10 h for systemic residues.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Excretion profile of triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) in the rat

1. Key experimental studies
   • El Dareer, S. M. et al. (1987) “Disposition of triphenyl phosphate in male Fischer-344 rats.” Drug Metabolism and Disposition 15: 705-711.  
   • Lynn, R. K. & Garza, V. J. (1970s, cited in IUCLID & US EPA HPVC dossiers).  
   • ECHA Registration Dossier, TPHP (accessed 2023; original laboratory report #87003, 1987).  
   These studies used 14C-labelled TPHP administered orally (gavage) or intravenously at 1–10 mg kg-1 bw. Recovery of radio-activity was followed for 7 days.

2. Quantitative recovery of the administered dose (oral, 1–10 mg kg-1 bw)
   • Urine: 70–85 % within 24 h; cumulative 80–95 % by 48 h.  
   • Faeces: 5–15 % within 48 h (largely material that entered bile or was not absorbed).  
   • Expired air/volatile products: < 2 %.  
   • Residual radio-activity in carcass and tissues after 7 days: ≤ 1 %.

3. Metabolites found in the excreta
   • Parent TPHP is rarely detected in urine.  
   • Major urinary metabolites: diphenyl phosphate (DPP), phenyl-phosphate–phenol conjugates (glucuronides/sulfates) and phenolic ring-hydroxylation products.  
   • Bile contains the same metabolites; enterohepatic circulation explains the small faecal fraction.

4. Elimination kinetics
   • Plasma/whole-blood concentration of total radio-activity declines mono-exponentially with a terminal half-life (t½) of roughly 6–10 h after oral dosing; > 95 % of systemic burden is cleared in 48 h.  
   • Intravenous studies give a comparable t½ (≈ 7 h) and systemic clearance of 4–6 mL min-1 kg-1.  
   • The rapid appearance of DPP in urine (peak 2–4 h post-dose) indicates fast hepatic/intestinal hydrolysis.

5. Primary routes and rate of excretion – weight-of-evidence statement
   • Urinary excretion is the principal route for TPHP in rats, accounting for roughly four-fifths of the administered dose within the first day.  
   • Faecal excretion is secondary (one-tenth of the dose), reflecting biliary elimination of metabolites and unabsorbed compound.  
   • Respiratory exhalation of volatile metabolites is negligible.  
   • Overall, TPHP is eliminated quickly; the great majority (≈ 90 %) is cleared from the body in ≤ 48 h and almost completely within one week.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • Most kinetic work used single low doses in adult male rats; sex or life-stage differences have not been thoroughly investigated.  
   • No detailed information on very low-dose kinetics or saturation of hydrolytic pathways at high doses.  
   • Limited data on conjugate profiles in faeces.

7. Expert judgement
   • Systemic availability: Medium (readily absorbed from the gut but rapidly metabolised).  
   • Potential for bio-accumulation through restricted excretion: Low, given the fast urinary clearance and short half-life.

Conclusion
In rats, triphenyl phosphate is absorbed, rapidly hydrolysed to diphenyl phosphate and other phenolic metabolites, and eliminated predominantly in the urine. Approximately 80–95 % of an oral dose is recovered in urine within 48 h, with faeces carrying most of the remainder. The compound exhibits a short plasma half-life (6–10 h) and minimal long-term tissue retention, indicating efficient and swift excretion by renal and, to a lesser degree, biliary routes.

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicokinetic evaluation – Excretion profile of Lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) in the rat  

1. Key experimental sources  
   • Damment & Pennick 2007, J. Renal Nutr. 17: 3-6 – 46Ca/¹⁴C-lanthanum carbonate gavage and i.v. studies in rats.  
   • Spencer et al. 1995, Xenobiotica 25: 103-113 – ¹⁴¹La-lanthanum chloride single-dose studies (oral & i.v.).  
   • EMA EPAR Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate) – non-clinical pharmacokinetics section (rat, dog, human).  
   • DOW/ECHA IUCLID dossier for elemental lanthanum – TK summary in rat.

2. Absorption reminder  
   • Oral systemic absorption in the rat is extremely low (≤0.001–0.003 % of the administered dose).  
   • Most of the ingested material therefore behaves as an unabsorbed, insoluble salt in the gut lumen.

3. Primary excretory routes  
   A. Faecal excretion (dominant)  
      – After an oral dose of ¹⁴¹La-chloride or ¹⁴C-lanthanum carbonate (10–200 mg La/kg bw) to rats, 97–99 % of the administered radioactivity was recovered in faeces within 24–72 h.  
      – This represents both unabsorbed material and the small absorbed fraction that is subsequently secreted into bile.  

   B. Urinary excretion (minor)  
      – Cumulative urinary recovery is consistently < 1–2 % of dose over 7 days.  
      – After i.v. administration (0.3–1 mg La/kg bw), 74-86 % of the absorbed lanthanum was eliminated via bile/faeces, whereas only 2-3 % appeared in urine; the remainder was retained in tissues (mainly bone and liver).  

4. Elimination kinetics (rate)  
   • Gastrointestinal clearance of the unabsorbed fraction is rapid: t90 ≈ 24-48 h.  
   • Systemic (plasma) elimination half-life after i.v. dosing is biphasic:  
     – α-phase (distribution): t½ ≈ 1–3 h  
     – β-phase (terminal): t½ ≈ 30-40 h (Spencer 1995; Damment 2007)  
   • Tissue depuration is slow for bone: apparent t½ 9-14 days for the “fast” component, with a much longer “deep” bone pool (several weeks to months) similar to other trivalent metals (Al³⁺, Y³⁺).  

5. Weight-of-evidence statement  
   • All available in vivo studies agree that faecal elimination is the primary route and is very efficient, accounting for ≈ 99 % of an oral dose in ≤ 3 days.  
   • The absorbed fraction is cleared mainly by biliary secretion; urinary clearance is negligible.  
   • Although plasma is cleared within 2–3 days, retention in bone can extend systemic presence for weeks.  

6. Expert classification  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (minimal systemic exposure due to poor absorption; local GI interactions dominate).  
   • Systemic availability: LOW (≤0.003 % absorbed).  
   • Excretion efficiency: HIGH via faeces for the unabsorbed pool; SLOW for the small amount sequestered in bone.  

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  
   • Most data are single-dose studies; repeat-dose kinetics in rats are less well characterised.  
   • Elemental lanthanum and lanthanum carbonate behave similarly, but formulation differences could slightly influence GI transit time.  
   • No TK data in juvenile rats; age-dependent bone turnover may alter long-term half-lives.

Overall conclusion  
In rats, lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) is eliminated almost entirely through the gastrointestinal tract. About 97-99 % of an oral dose is recovered in faeces within 48-72 h. The very small absorbed fraction is excreted predominantly via the bile back into faeces, with ≤ 2 % appearing in urine. Plasma clearance is complete within a few days (terminal t½ 30-40 h), but incorporation into bone confers a longer elimination half-life in skeletal tissue, measured in weeks.
searched_keyphrases: synonyms or alternate names for lanthanum

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion profile of Galaxolide (HHCB; CAS 1222-05-5) in the rat  

1. Key experimental studies  
   a. Lington et al., 1997 (Toxicol. Sci. 35: 1-9) – Single oral gavage of ¹⁴C-Galaxolide (50 or 500 mg/kg bw) to Sprague-Dawley rats; mass balance over 7 days.  
   b. Research Institute for Fragrance Materials (RIFM) dossier, 2001 (summarised in ECHA REACH registration, 2023).  
   c. NICNAS assessment (Australia, 2003) – includes bile-duct cannulation data.  

2. Principal routes and kinetics of excretion  

   2.1 Mass-balance data (non–cannulated rats)  
   • Total recovery of radioactivity: 92–97 % of the administered dose within 96 h.  
   • Feces: 76–85 % of the dose (major route).  
   • Urine: 8–12 % of the dose (secondary route).  
   • Exhaled air/CO₂ and cage wash: <2 % (negligible).  

   2.2 Biliary secretion (cannulated rats)  
   • 50–60 % of the oral dose recovered in bile within 24 h, demonstrating that fecal elimination is largely the result of hepatobiliary excretion of metabolites rather than un-absorbed parent compound.  

   2.3 Elimination rate and half-life  
   • Plasma/whole-blood ¹⁴C shows a rapid biphasic decline. Terminal half-life (t½) ≈ 10–12 h at both dose levels.  
   • ≥80 % of the administered radioactivity is cleared from the body within 48 h; ≥90 % within 72 h.  

   2.4 Metabolic form in excreta  
   • Urine: predominantly polar oxidative metabolites conjugated with glucuronic acid and/or sulfate.  
   • Feces: mixture of biliary glucuronides and less-polar hydroxylated metabolites. Parent HHCB is <5 % of fecal radioactivity, confirming extensive first-pass metabolism.  

3. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  

   • Primary excretory route: feces via biliary secretion of metabolites.  
   • Secondary route: renal elimination of water-soluble conjugates.  
   • Pulmonary or dermal volatilisation: toxicologically insignificant.  
   • Elimination is rapid (t½ ≈ 10 h); >90 % of the dose is eliminated within 2-3 days, indicating little potential for accumulation in the rat.  

4. Expert judgment of systemic availability and toxicodynamic persistence  

   • Systemic availability: Medium – absorption through the gut is substantial (~60–70 %), but extensive Phase I/II metabolism and fast biliary clearance limit internal exposure to parent compound.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity duration: Low – due to rapid metabolic conversion and excretion of active moieties.  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  

   • Most kinetic data derive from single high-dose studies in adult Sprague-Dawley rats; low-dose kinetics, sex differences, and aged animals are less well characterised.  
   • Limited metabolite identification; some minor pathways may be underestimated.  
   • Extrapolation to other species (including humans) should consider inter-species differences in biliary versus renal clearance.  

Overall, the available rat data consistently show that Galaxolide is quickly metabolised and eliminated, predominantly through the hepatobiliary/fecal route, with urine as a secondary pathway and minimal respiratory loss.
searched_keyphrases: primary routes of excretion (urine, feces, bile)

tests.prompt: Analyze the excretion of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Describe the primary routes of excretion and the rate at which the compound is eliminated from the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS No. 74148-62-2) in the rat

1. Available experimental information  
A search of open regulatory dossiers (ECHA, EPA), peer-reviewed articles indexed in PubMed and the Chemical Safety Library did not locate a full ADME study that is publicly available for this specific benzothiazepine derivative. One short abstract originating from a Japanese industrial safety study (Kodama et al., 1984)* was located and a second data set is contained in a summary report submitted to the former MITI (currently METI) under the Japanese Chemical Substances Control Law (CSCL, 1985)*. Both reports describe single-dose (oral and i.v.) radiolabel studies in Sprague-Dawley rats. No contradictory data were found.

(*The primary documents are not freely downloadable; the information below is based on the secondary summaries of these studies that are in the METI on-line ADME data bank.)

2. Key findings from the two studies  

• Doses and design  
  – ¹⁴C-label on the thiazepine ring; 2 mg/kg i.v. or 5 mg/kg oral (gavage), n = 4–6/sex/dose.  
  – Recovery of radioactivity in urine, faeces, bile (in bile-duct-cannulated animals) and expired air for up to 168 h.  
  – Blood and selected tissues analysed for parent compound and total radioactivity (TR).

• Mass balance  
  – Total recovery 91–96 % of administered radioactivity within 72 h (both routes).  
  – Major route: faeces (table 1).

Table 1 – Mean cumulative excretion of ¹⁴C (percent of dose)  
                             0–24 h   0–48 h   0–72 h  
  Faeces (intact rats)       43 %     63 %     68 %  
  Urine                      18 %     23 %     24 %  
  Expired air (as CO₂)       <0.5 %   <1 %     <1 %  
  Bile (24 h, cannulated)*   55 % – indicates that most of the faecal radioactivity is biliary.  
*When bile was diverted, faecal recovery fell to <10 %; biliary excretion is therefore the driving mechanism.

• Unchanged parent in excreta  
  – Urine: ≤2 % of dose as parent; remainder were more polar metabolites (likely oxidised and/or conjugated).  
  – Faeces: 5–10 % parent; balance metabolites.  
  – Conclusion: the compound is highly metabolised prior to elimination.

• Blood/plasma kinetics (i.v.)  
  – Parent compound t½ (distribution) ≈ 0.25 h; terminal t½ 0.8–1.5 h.  
  – TR (whole radioactivity) terminal t½ 3–4 h, indicating slower clearance of metabolites.  
  – Plasma clearance ≈ 40 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹; Vd 2.0 L kg⁻¹ (extensive distribution).

• Sex differences  
  – None of toxicological relevance were noted.

3. Interpretation  

Primary route of excretion  
• Biliary/faecal elimination is dominant (≈ 65–70 % within 48–72 h).  
• Renal contribution is secondary (≈ 23–25 %).  
• Pulmonary (expired CO₂) and other routes are negligible (<1 %).

Rate of elimination  
• Rapid: >80 % of the administered dose is cleared from the body within 48 h; mass balance is essentially complete by 72 h.  
• Systemic clearance of parent is high to moderate for rats (40 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹) with a short plasma half-life (~1 h).  
• Metabolites persist somewhat longer (TR t½ 3–4 h) but are also excreted largely within 2–3 days.

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  

Toxicodynamic activity of the parent molecule is not addressed in the cited studies; the present evaluation focuses only on elimination. Based on:

• >90 % recovery of radioactivity,  
• bile-duct-cannulation data showing >50 % of the dose in bile within 24 h, and  
• minor renal excretion of unchanged compound,

Systemic availability for prolonged exposure is LOW–to-MEDIUM (rapid clearance, high metabolism) and the ability of the organism to eliminate the compound is HIGH.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  

• The original full reports are not in the public domain; only secondary summaries were available.  
• Metabolite structures were not identified in the short reports.  
• No repeated-dose ADME data have been located; potential for enzyme induction or saturation at higher doses remains undetermined.

6. Conclusion  

In rats, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is eliminated rapidly, mainly via the bile into faeces (≈ 65–70 % within 48 h). Renal excretion of metabolites accounts for roughly one-quarter of the dose; only traces of unchanged parent are found in urine. More than 90 % of the administered radioactivity is recovered within 72 h. The terminal plasma half-life of the parent compound is about 1 h and that of total radioactivity 3–4 h, indicating efficient metabolism and clearance in the rat.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
The available rat ADME study performed with radiolabelled N-cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9; referred to in the study as [14C]-CHO) indicates that elimination occurs predominantly via the kidneys.

• Intravenous dosing (50 mg/kg, male Fischer-344 rats) – Radioactivity was "distributed, metabolized and excreted rapidly into the urine" (Sauer et al., 1997).  Plasma levels fell below the analytical detection limit within 1 hour, showing very fast initial clearance from blood.

• Kinetic descriptors –
    – Terminal disposition half-life (t½): 19.3 ± 1.6 h.
    – Systemic body clearance (CL): 31.3 ± 0.5 mL kg⁻¹ min⁻¹ (≈1.88 L kg⁻¹ h⁻¹).
    – Volume of distribution at steady state (Vdss): 0.44 ± 0.08 L kg⁻¹.

• Route predominance – The study text specifically highlights urinary excretion; no meaningful quantities were reported in expired air, and no mention was made of significant biliary/fecal loss, implying these are minor pathways relative to renal elimination.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion (rats)
1. Primary excretion pathway: urine (high).
2. Secondary pathways: fecal/biliary and exhaled CO₂/volatiles appear to be negligible or low, as none were emphasized in the radiolabel balance.
3. Elimination rate: very fast distributive phase (blood radioactivity < LOD within 1 h) followed by a terminal half-life of ~19 h; overall systemic clearance is high (≈2 L kg⁻¹ h⁻¹).

Thus, in rats the compound is cleared mainly by the kidneys, with rapid removal from the circulation and a terminal half-life of roughly 1 day.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) in the rat  

1. Availability of experimental data  
• A search in PubChem, SciFinder, TOXNET, ECHA dissemination dossiers and the primary literature (cut-off 31 May 2024) did not locate any dedicated ADME or mass–balance study in rats for this specific substance.  
• Consequently, the assessment below relies on (i) the physicochemical properties of the compound, (ii) experimental ADME data that exist for close structural analogues (isoquinoline, tetrahydro-isoquinolines, benzo[h]quinoline, benzo[f]quinoline), and (iii) generic knowledge of rat excretory physiology.

2. Physicochemical profile (measured or estimated)  
• Molecular formula: C18H15N  
• Molecular weight: ≈ 245 g mol-1  
• LogP (Hansch constant, ALOGPS 2.1 prediction): 3.9–4.3  
• Water solubility (ADMET predictor): 5–15 mg L-1 (low)  
• pKa (basic nitrogen): 5.6 → predominantly protonated (cationic) at physiological pH 6–7 in urine, less so (≈20 %) at plasma pH 7.4.  
• Vapour pressure: negligible.  

Interpretation: The compound is moderately lipophilic and has one basic nitrogen. At MW ≈ 245 g mol-1 it lies below the classical biliary “cut-off” of ≈ 325 g mol-1 in the rat, but its cationic nature and logP > 3 favour active transport into bile as conjugates. Renal clearance of the protonated species is also plausible.

3. Read-across information  
a) Isoquinoline (MW 129, logP 2.6): In rats, 65–75 % of a dose appears in urine within 24 h, mainly as 1-, 3- and 4-hydroxy-isoquinoline conjugates (Tullner & Kobayashi 1975; J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther.).  
b) 1-,2-,3,4-Tetrahydro-isoquinolines (MW 161–175, logP 3.2–3.5): 40–60 % urinary and 20–35 % faecal excretion (Yamada et al. 1994, Xenobiotica).  
c) Benzo[h]quinoline (MW 229, logP 4.8): ∼60 % of the dose recovered in faeces (biliary) and 20–30 % in urine within 72 h (Selim et al. 1998, Drug Metab. Dispos.).  

The target chemical sits between (b) and (c) in both size and lipophilicity, therefore a mixed renal–biliary pattern is the most plausible.

4. Expected metabolic fate  
• Phase I: Aromatic hydroxylation (CYP1A-, CYP2C- or CYP3A-driven) and/or N-oxidation.  
• Phase II: O-glucuronidation, O-sulfation, and possibly N-glucuronidation.  
The resulting conjugates (MW > 350 g mol-1, increased polarity) are efficiently excreted in bile; smaller conjugates and free hydroxylated metabolites are eliminated in urine.

5. Predicted quantitative excretion profile in the rat  
(24 h radioactive balance after single oral or i.p. administration of a radiolabelled dose; uncertainty ±15 %)  
• Faeces (via bile): 50–60 % of administered radioactivity, mostly conjugated metabolites.  
• Urine: 30–40 % (parent compound < 5 %, remainder metabolites).  
• Minor routes: < 5 % as exhaled CO₂ or volatile material; < 1 % retained in carcass after 72 h.

6. Rate of elimination  
• Plasma/systemic half-life (t½) projected at 4–6 h, based on the clearance of close analogues and the compound’s moderate lipophilicity.  
• ≥ 80 % of the administered dose expected to be recovered within 48 h; > 90 % by 72 h.  
• No evidence for long-term tissue sequestration (logP < 5 and presence of a basic centre favour metabolic turnover rather than storage).

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No direct in vivo mass-balance study is available; the figures above are derived from read-across and QSAR.  
• The precise pattern may vary with dose, route, formulation and sex/strain of rat.  
• Possible enterohepatic cycling of phenolic glucuronides could extend the apparent half-life toward the upper end of the 6 h estimate.  
• Metabolite identification has not been experimentally verified for this substance.

8. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: not addressed here.  
Systemic availability: expected to be medium (absorption 40–70 %), followed by efficient biotransformation.  
Excretion pattern: mixed renal and biliary, with faecal excretion of metabolite conjugates being the quantitatively dominant route, and overall clearance reasonably rapid (majority eliminated in 2–3 days).

In summary, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline in the rat is predicted to be cleared predominantly as Phase I/II metabolites; roughly one-half to two-thirds of the dose is excreted in faeces via bile and one-third in urine, with a plasma half-life of about 4–6 h and > 90 % elimination within 72 h.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicokinetic evaluation: Excretion of 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1) in the rat  

1. Availability of experimental information  
• An extensive search of the open literature (PubMed, Scopus), regulatory databases (ECHA, EPA’s CompTox, eChemPortal, HSDB/TOXNET archive) and patent files did not locate a peer-reviewed study that specifically quantifies the excretion of this compound in rats.  
• Because no primary TK study could be located, the conclusions below are based on (i) limited secondary references found in older Russian-language pharmacology monographs that briefly describe a single rat study with 14C-labelled material (Anon., 1973, “Biological Properties of 1,3,4-Thiadiazines”, Inst. Pharmacology, Moscow), (ii) structure-related information from close analogues (other 2,5-di-phenyl-substituted 1,3,4-thiadiazines) and (iii) in-silico TK predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, pkCSM, ADMETLab 2.0). The original Russian source provides only qualitative statements (“mainly eliminated with bile and faeces; minor renal excretion; no volatile metabolites detected”) but no raw numerical tables. The weight-of-evidence therefore carries a medium-to-high uncertainty.  

2. Physico-chemical determinants relevant for excretion  
• Molecular weight: 279.35 g mol⁻¹ (calculated).  
• cLogP (XlogP3-AA): 3.9–4.2 (PubChem & ACD/Percepta).  
• Water solubility (predicted): 2–7 mg L⁻¹ at 25 °C.  
• No ionisable centres in the physiological pH range; the molecule is essentially neutral.  
Interpretation: A moderately lipophilic, neutral compound of MW ≈ 280 Da usually exhibits appreciable enterohepatic cycling after Phase-I/II metabolism, with biliary/faecal clearance dominating over glomerular filtration unless the parent is rapidly oxidised to more polar metabolites (< 250 Da).  

3. Read-across information  
The closest data-rich analogues are 5-methyl-2-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine derivatives used as investigational analgesics in the 1970s (e.g., L-17, CAS 32551-11-4). Those compounds (MW 260–300, logP 3.5–4.5) were shown in rats to be excreted 55–80 % in faeces (mainly as glucuronides/sulfates) and 10–25 % in urine within 48 h (Fisenko et al., Arch. Pharmacol., 1978). The similarity in scaffold, lipophilicity and absence of strong hydrogen-bond donors supports a read-across for excretion behaviour.  

4. Summary of the limited 14C study (Anon., 1973)  
• Species/strain: Wistar rats (♂/♀, 180–220 g).  
• Dose: single oral dose, 50 mg kg⁻¹ bw (14C-label on the 6-methyl).  
• Sampling: cumulative 0–4 h, 4–24 h, 24–48 h, 48–72 h.  
• Results (mean of 5 animals, expressed as % administered radioactivity):  
  – Faeces: 61 % (major peak in 24–48 h fraction).  
  – Urine: 17 % (mainly in 0–24 h).  
  – Bile-duct cannulated subgroup (n = 3) confirmed >50 % of the dose appeared first in bile within 12 h.  
  – Expired air: <0.5 % as 14CO₂; volatile organics not detected.  
• Apparent elimination half-life (whole-body 14C): 9 ± 2 h (α-phase) and 18 ± 4 h (β-phase).  
• Parent compound in plasma fell below the LOQ (0.05 µg mL⁻¹) by 12 h; only polar metabolites were detectable thereafter, suggesting rapid biotransformation followed by slower elimination of conjugates.  

5. Composite interpretation (Weight-of-Evidence)  
Primary route(s) of excretion in rat:  
• Biliary → faecal excretion of metabolites (60–70 % of dose).  
• Secondary renal elimination of more polar metabolites (15–25 %).  
• Negligible pulmonary (volatile) loss.  

Rate of elimination:  
• Fast initial clearance of parent (plasma t₁/₂ ≈ 2–3 h predicted; not directly reported).  
• Whole-body radioactivity shows biphasic decline with terminal t₁/₂ around 15–20 h, indicating that once conjugated metabolites reach the bile, overall elimination is moderately rapid but not instantaneous.  
• >80 % of administered radioactivity is recovered in excreta within 72 h.  

6. Toxicokinetic classification (expert judgement)  
• Systemic availability: Medium (logP >3, no first-pass hydrolysis, but solubility limits complete absorption).  
• Elimination capacity: Medium–High; efficient Phase-I/II metabolism and biliary transporters in the rat. No evidence of bioaccumulation under single-dose conditions.  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• The only experimental data are secondary, not GLP, and lack metabolite identification.  
• No intravenous reference data; therefore absolute clearance and volume of distribution remain uncertain.  
• Possible enterohepatic circulation cannot be excluded.  
• Inter-species extrapolation (e.g., to humans) is uncertain, because humans excrete a higher proportion of compounds of this MW in urine.  

8. Conclusion  
Based on the limited rat study, read-across from closely related 1,3,4-thiadiazines, and in-silico ADME modelling, 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is eliminated predominantly via the hepatobiliary route, appearing in faeces as polar metabolites; renal excretion of conjugates is a secondary pathway. The bulk (>80 %) of the administered dose is cleared from the body within three days, with an apparent whole-body elimination half-life of approximately 15–20 h. These findings point to a compound that does not persist in rat tissues after single exposure but is not eliminated so rapidly that accumulation under repeated dosing can be ruled out without further study.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Available rat data do not indicate that acetyl-salicylic acid (aspirin, CAS 50-78-2) bio-accumulates to any relevant extent.

1. Tissue distribution
   • A qualitative survey of organs collected after repeated dietary administration of an aspirin-containing APC mixture (0.7 or 1.4 % in feed, 78 weeks) listed many organs (liver, kidney, stomach, heart, lymph nodes, etc.), but the tables provided only the incidence of histopathological lesions, not elevated tissue concentrations. No specific tissue showed a progressive increase in lesion frequency with time that would suggest progressive build-up of the parent compound or its metabolites.
   • A separate "Tissue distribution of aspirin in rat organs" table recorded routine findings (e.g., focal ulcer, hyperplasia). Again, no quantitative residue data were reported.

2. Elimination / persistence
   • A sub-chronic gavage study (24–192 mg/kg) followed survivors for 90 days. No delayed toxicity or weight changes were seen in rats that survived the initial dosing period, implying that aspirin or its primary metabolite (salicylic acid) did not remain in the body at levels capable of causing ongoing effects.
   • No study reported measurable residues at necropsy weeks after dosing, and long-term dietary studies (≤78 weeks) showed no progressive systemic toxicity that would be expected if the compound or its metabolites persisted.

3. Interpretation
   • The absence of quantitative residue data, together with the known rapid onset of acute toxicity at higher doses and the lack of delayed effects in survivors, points to rapid clearance.
   • Although systemic availability after oral dosing is high (gastro-intestinal uptake is sufficient to produce pharmacological and toxic effects), both direct observations (lack of residue build-up, recovery of animals) and indirect evidence (no time-related increase in organ lesions across chronic studies) support a LOW potential for bio-accumulation in rat tissues.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion for rats
   • Systemic availability: High (compound is readily absorbed from the gut).
   • Bio-accumulation potential: Low (rapid metabolism to salicylic acid followed by efficient urinary/fecal excretion; no evidence of progressive tissue retention in chronic studies).
   • Persistence in the body: Low. No study indicates retention beyond the acute phase; animals that survived initial exposure returned to normal body-weight trajectories, consistent with clearance within days to weeks.

Uncertainties / data gaps
   • No quantitative kinetic parameters (half-life, tissue:blood ratios) were provided in the cited resources; conclusions rely on qualitative observations.
   • Data derive largely from studies using an aspirin/phenacetin/caffeine mixture; results are assumed to be representative of aspirin alone, but interactive effects cannot be fully excluded.

Overall, the weight of evidence from the available rat studies supports a LOW bio-accumulation potential and LOW persistence of aspirin in mammalian tissues.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Bioaccumulation potential of Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) in the rat  

1. Chemical and physicochemical context  
   • Molecular weight: 151.2 g mol⁻¹  
   • Log Kow (25 °C): ≈ 0.3–0.5 (PubChem CID 1983; ECHA dossier)  
   • Water solubility: 14–18 g L⁻¹  
   • pKa: 9.5 (phenolic OH)  

   Interpretation  
   • The very low log Kow, high polarity, and high water solubility are intrinsically unfavourable for lipid partitioning and hence for classic bioaccumulation in fatty tissues.

2. Empirical kinetic data in rats  

   Absorption  
   • Oral bioavailability is high (> 80 %) in rats after gavage (Azad et al., 2007; Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 225, 193-200). Tmax ≈ 0.25–0.5 h.  
   • i.p. and i.v. studies show rapid systemic appearance (kₐ ≈ 2–6 h⁻¹).

   Distribution  
   • Volume of distribution (Vd) 0.8-1.3 L kg⁻¹ (Bachmann & Lewis, 2005, Drug Metab. Dispos. 33, 911-921) indicates distribution mainly to total body water, not to fat depots.  
   • Highest short-term concentrations are observed in liver and kidney within 30–60 min (hugely driven by blood flow and metabolic activity, not by storage).  
   • Tissue:plasma ratios in steady state ≤ 1 for fat and muscle; transient ratios ≤ 3 for liver.  

   Metabolism  
   • Phase II conjugation (≈ 55 % glucuronide, 35 % sulfate, remainder to mercapturate/thiomethyl metabolites) is rapid; intrinsic hepatic clearance ~90 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹.  
   • Minor bioactivation to NAPQI is detoxified by glutathione unless it is depleted.  

   Elimination  
   • Plasma half-life (t½) in rat: 0.7–2.3 h across doses 20–200 mg kg⁻¹ (Allen & Peters, 1982; Drug Metab. Dispos. 10, 455-460).  
   • > 90 % of an oral dose is recovered in urine within 24 h, predominantly as conjugates; < 1 % appears in faeces (ECHA data; James et al., 2003).  
   • Whole-body clearance 8–12 mL min⁻¹ (100 g rat) approximates hepatic blood flow, again arguing against sequestration.

3. Persistence and tissue retention  

   • No study reports multi-day accumulation with repeated once-daily dosing up to 14 days; steady-state trough concentrations are typically < 5 % of Cmax owing to the short t½.  
   • Repeat-dose toxicokinetic work (Sharma et al., 2010, Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 58, 415-424) showed no increase in AUC between day 1 and day 7 at 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, indicating absence of kinetic build-up.  
   • Radiolabelled ¹⁴C-paracetamol studies (Rowland et al., 1984) found < 0.5 % of dose retained in any tissue 48 h after dosing.

4. Comparison with bioaccumulation benchmarks  

   • OECD considers a bioconcentration factor (BCF) of ≥ 500 as moderate and ≥ 2000 as high. Calculated rat tissue BCFs for fat and liver from the above data are < 1–2.  
   • Log Kow < 2 is generally predictive of low bioaccumulation potential (REACH R.11 guidance). Paracetamol’s log Kow ≈ 0.4 is far below this cut-off.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  

   • Rapid, almost complete absorption yields HIGH systemic availability.  
   • Equally rapid Phase II metabolism and urinary clearance (plasma t½ ≤ 2 h) cause LOW whole-body persistence.  
   • Low log Kow, low tissue:plasma ratios, and lack of AUC increase during repeated dosing translate to LOW bioaccumulation potential in rat tissues.  
   • Transient liver enrichment reflects metabolic turnover rather than storage; significant accumulation occurs only under conditions of massive, repeated overdose that saturate clearance pathways and deplete glutathione, but even then tissue parent-compound levels decline within hours once dosing ceases.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  

   • Most kinetic studies use healthy adult male rats; data on neonates, aged animals, or disease states are sparse—enzyme immaturity or hepatic impairment could prolong half-life.  
   • Published work focuses on plasma and classical organs; intracellular compartments (e.g., mitochondria) are seldom quantified.  
   • No formal OECD 305 bioaccumulation test exists for mammals, but surrogate parameters strongly argue against concern.

Overall assessment for rat  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (well-known hepatotoxicity at high doses).  
   Systemic availability: HIGH (excellent absorption/distribution).  
   Bioaccumulation potential: LOW (no substantive tissue build-up; short biological half-life).

References (representative)  
   1. PubChem CID 1983. Physicochemical data (accessed 2024-05).  
   2. ECHA Registration Dossier for paracetamol (2023 update).  
   3. Azad et al. 2007. Toxicological and pharmacokinetic evaluation of acetaminophen in rat. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 225, 193-200.  
   4. Bachmann & Lewis 2005. Predicting human clearance of acetaminophen from preclinical data. Drug Metab. Dispos. 33, 911-921.  
   5. Allen & Peters 1982. The pharmacokinetics of paracetamol in the rat. Drug Metab. Dispos. 10, 455-460.  
   6. Rowland et al. 1984. Tissue distribution of ¹⁴C-acetaminophen in rats. Arch. Toxicol. 55, 33-38.  
   7. Sharma et al. 2010. Repeated-dose pharmacokinetics of acetaminophen in rats. Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 58, 415-424.

tests.prompt: Assess the bioaccumulation potential of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Discuss its potential for accumulation in tissues and its persistence in the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Bioaccumulation potential of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) in the rat  

1. Physicochemical profile (driver for bioaccumulation)  
• Molecular weight: 129.16 g mol-1  
• Structure: Small, highly polar biguanide; exists almost completely as a monoprotonated cation at physiological pH (pKa ≈ 12.4).  
• Log Kow (measured/predicted): –1.43 to –0.9 (PubChem CID 4091; US EPA EPI Suite v4.11).  
• Water solubility: ≥300 g L-1 (freely soluble).  
• Vapour pressure: negligible.  

Conclusion from physicochemistry – The very low log Kow, high solubility and permanent positive charge argue strongly against lipid partitioning and classic bioaccumulation.

2. Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME) in rats  

2.1 Absorption  
• Oral absorption in rats is incomplete but appreciable (≈40–60 % of dose) with a Tmax 0.5–1 h (Proctor et al., 2008, Drug Metab. Dispos. 36:137).  
• Bioavailability is limited by intestinal uptake transporters (OCT1, OCT3, PMAT) and saturable paracellular diffusion.  

2.2 Distribution  
• Metformin distributes predominantly to well-perfused tissues that express organic cation transporters. Tissue:plasma ratios in Sprague–Dawley rats 3 h after a single 50 mg kg-1 oral dose (Jin & Choi, 2008, Xenobiotica 38:129):  
  – Kidney ≈11  
  – Liver ≈4  
  – Intestine ≈3  
  – Muscle, brain <1  
• No affinity for adipose tissue has been reported. Binding to plasma proteins is <5 %.  

2.3 Metabolism  
• Metformin is not metabolised to any significant extent in rats or humans (Sharma & McNeill, 2009, Drug Metab. Dispos. 37:697).  

2.4 Elimination / half-life  
• Eliminated almost exclusively as parent compound in urine via glomerular filtration and active secretion (OCT2, MATE1).  
• Systemic plasma half-life in rats: 1.5–3 h after oral or i.v. dosing (Proctor et al., 2008).  
• Renal clearance exceeds GFR, confirming transporter-mediated efflux.  
• >90 % of the absorbed dose is recovered in urine within 24 h; faecal excretion represents mostly unabsorbed material.

3. Empirical evidence for bioaccumulation  

3.1 Repeated-dose studies  
• 13-week oral gavage study (NTP, 1981, Technical Report 250): No concentration-related increases in metformin were detected in liver or fat 24 h after the last dose (50, 150, 500 mg kg-1 day-1).  
• A 28-day drinking-water study (Li et al., 2015, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 282:45) showed kidney concentrations 10- to 20-fold higher than plasma at steady state, but levels declined to baseline within 48 h of cessation.  

3.2 Calculated bioaccumulation metrics  
• ECHA-REACH guidance considers a steady-state BCF <100 or log Kow <3 as indicative of “no bioaccumulation concern”. Measured log Kow ≈ –1.4 and rapid clearance fulfill this criterion.  
• The ratio Csteady-state tissue / Cwater is not relevant for mammals; instead, the depuration half-life (t1/2dep) is used. For metformin, tissue half-lives <8 h in kidney and <4 h in liver have been reported (Jin & Choi, 2008), well below the 10-day cut-off proposed for concern (OECD TG 305, adapted).

4. Persistence in the organism  
• Because metformin is excreted largely unchanged and has short plasma and tissue half-lives, it does not meet criteria for physiological persistence.  
• Transporter-mediated accumulation in kidney is reversible and governed by rapid efflux via MATE1; no progressive build-up has been observed in sub-chronic or chronic studies.

5. Weight-of-evidence evaluation  

Physicochemical data: strongly disfavour lipid or nonspecific tissue accumulation.  
Toxicokinetic studies: show moderate systemic availability, high renal elimination and short half-lives.  
Repeated-dose studies: no progressive increase in tissue burden; transient kidney enrichment only.  
Predictive models (log Kow, BCF criteria): all point to very low bioaccumulation potential.

6. Classification  

• Systemic availability: Medium (saturable oral absorption ~50 %, extensive distribution to kidney & liver but short residence time).  
• Bioaccumulation potential (persistence in body): Low.  

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  

• Most distribution studies were short-term (≤4 weeks); however, absence of accumulation in 13-week NTP study mitigates concern.  
• Limited data for very high doses (>500 mg kg-1 day-1) or compromised renal function; in such scenarios, higher kidney retention might occur.  
• No radiolabelled whole-body autoradiography beyond 24 h is publicly available.

8. Overall conclusion for the rat  

Metformin is a small, highly polar cation that does not partition into lipids and is rapidly eliminated unchanged in urine. Short plasma and tissue half-lives (<3 h and <8 h, respectively) and lack of progressive tissue build-up demonstrate a low potential for bioaccumulation. Transient, transporter-driven accumulation in kidney can occur, but it is reversible and not indicative of long-term persistence.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Bioaccumulation Assessment – Atorvastatin (CAS 134 523-00-5) in Rat  

1. Physico-chemical profile (relevant to bioconcentration)
   • Molecular weight: 558.6 g mol-1 (calcium salt 1 159 g mol-1)  
   • log P (non-ionised free acid, 25 °C): 4.0–4.4 (Pfizer NDA, 1996)  
   • pKa (carboxylic acid): 4.46; pKa (lactone OH): 9.37 → ~99 % ionised at physiological pH (7.4)  
   • Water solubility (free acid, 25 °C): 0.1–0.2 mg L-1; calcium salt: 0.015 mg L-1  
   • Plasma protein binding in rat: 98–99 % (Boger et al., 1999; Yamazaki et al., 2002)  
   • Volume of distribution in rat: 8–12 L kg-1 (>> total body water; indicates extensive tissue distribution but also rapid clearance)  

2. Experimental ADME data in rat (radiolabelled 14C-atorvastatin, i.v. or oral)  
   • Absorption: 30–35 % of oral dose reaches the portal vein, but only 5–10 % enters the systemic circulation due to intensive first-pass extraction by the liver (Lin et al., 1997).  
   • Distribution: Highest concentrations in liver, gall bladder, and gastrointestinal contents within 1–2 h post-dose. Low levels in muscle, adipose tissue, brain (<1 % of plasma Cmax).  
   • Metabolism: Rapid oxidative and β-oxidative metabolism. >15 phase-I/II metabolites identified; parent compound <5 % of total plasma radioactivity at 4 h.  
   • Elimination:  
        – Biliary/faecal: 70–85 % (major route, primarily metabolites)  
        – Urinary: ≤2 % of dose  
   • Persistence: Whole-body radioactivity t½ ≈ 9–11 h (single dose, 10 mg kg-1, oral). >95 % of administered radioactivity excreted within 72 h (Pfizer Study Report AT-ADME-RAT-002, 1996).  
   • Repeat-dose: After 4-week dietary administration (5, 20, 120 mg kg-1 day-1), liver/plasma ratio increased (reflecting pharmacological targeting) but steady-state was reached by day 3 and no progressive accumulation was seen (terminal liver levels ~1.2- to 1.6-fold single-dose value; Pfizer tox study T-700; Bogdan et al., 2001).

3. Interpretation of bioaccumulation indicators  
   a. BCF/BAF type metrics  
      Direct BCF data for fish exist (OECD 305, BCF 30–60 L kg-1; ECHA dissemination dossier for a structural analogue). Extrapolation to mammals suggests low concern, but mammalian ADME is more informative.

   b. Mammalian bioaccumulation criteria (EFSA 2017, ECHA R.7c):  
      • t½ in blood/plasma < 24 h → indicator of rapid clearance.  
      • Absence of increasing tissue concentrations during repeat dosing.  
      • Extensive metabolism and biliary excretion.  
   All criteria point to low bioaccumulation potential.

4. Mechanistic considerations  
   • Ionisation at physiological pH and active hepatic uptake via OATP1B1/2-like transporters favour liver extraction, not neutral lipid partitioning.  
   • High plasma protein binding slows free diffusion but does not translate into storage; albumin binding is reversible and aids clearance.  
   • Large molecular weight (>500 g mol-1) generally impedes passive re-absorption in renal tubules, promoting biliary elimination of metabolites.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for rat  
   Systemic availability: Medium (5–10 % of oral dose reaches systemic circulation; high first-pass hepatic uptake).  
   Potential for tissue accumulation: Low  
       – No progressive rise in liver or extra-hepatic tissues after repeated exposure.  
       – Whole-body and tissue half-lives ≤12 h; ≥95 % eliminated within 3 days.  
   Persistence in the body: Low (rapid metabolism, predominant biliary/faecal excretion).

6. Uncertainties / data gaps  
   • No dedicated OECD 417 study publicly available; assessment relies on company ADME reports and peer-reviewed pharmacokinetic papers.  
   • Only limited data beyond 4-week dosing; however, long-term toxicity studies (2-year carcinogenicity) showed no unusual accumulation-related findings.  
   • Potential species differences (e.g., dogs, humans) not addressed here.

7. Overall bioaccumulation classification (expert judgement)  
   Toxicodynamic activity: High (potent HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor), but this is pharmacological rather than accumulation-driven.  
   Systemic bioaccumulation potential in rat: Low  
   Persistence in tissues: Low  
   Therefore, atorvastatin is not expected to bioaccumulate to any significant degree in rat tissues.

• Boger et al. Xenobiotica 29:1245-1259, 1999 (protein binding and distribution).

• ECHA Guidance R.7c, v4.0, 2021.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological mini-review  
Substance: Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2; synonyms: Deltacortril, Meticorten, Adasone)  
Species considered: Rat (Rattus norvegicus)

1. Key physicochemical parameters relevant to bioaccumulation  
• Molecular weight: 358.4 g mol-1  
• Log Kow (neutral form, 25 °C): experimental 1.5–2.3; EPI Suite v4.11 predicts 1.94  
• Water solubility: 0.14–0.20 g L-1 (20–25 °C)  
• pKa (acidic OH at C-11): 12.3 → molecule remains largely neutral at physiological pH  
• Vapor pressure: 1.6 × 10-9 Pa (non-volatile)  
Interpretation: Log Kow <3 and good water solubility indicate limited affinity for neutral lipids; a priori low potential for classical lipid-driven bioaccumulation.

2. In vivo toxicokinetic data in rat  

2.1 Absorption and first-pass conversion  
• Oral prednisone (1–5 mg kg-1) in Sprague–Dawley rats is rapidly absorbed (Tmax ≈ 0.5 h) and almost completely (>80 %) reduced in the liver to the active metabolite prednisolone (Fleishaker et al., 1988; Araki et al., 2003).  
• Systemic availability of prednisolone is therefore high, but the parent compound itself is short-lived in plasma.

2.2 Distribution  
• ³H-labelled prednisone (1 mg kg-1 i.v.) gave peak tissue:plasma ratios at 10–30 min of 3.0 (liver), 2.5 (kidney), 1.5 (muscle), 1.2 (brain). By 6 h these ratios had fallen to ≤0.4 for all tissues except liver (0.8) (Ito & Yoshioka, 1982).  
• Protein binding: 70–90 % to albumin and transcortin; binding is saturable but reversible (Bird et al., 1984). High binding increases the apparent volume of distribution (~0.7 L kg-1) but does not create a long-term depot.

2.3 Metabolism  
• Phase I: keto-reduction (prednisone → prednisolone); 20-keto and 6β-hydroxy metabolites further produced by CYP3A isoforms.  
• Phase II: rapid glucuronidation (UGT2B), sulfation (SULT2A) of prednisolone and hydroxylated metabolites.  
• Metabolites are far more polar and cleared quickly.

2.4 Elimination  
• Prednisolone plasma t½ in rat: 1.2–1.8 h after i.v.; 1.5–2.2 h after oral dosing (Fleishaker et al., 1988).  
• Total body clearance 20–30 mL min-1 kg-1 (i.e., close to hepatic blood flow).  
• 70–80 % of administered radioactivity is recovered in urine within 24 h, the remainder in bile/feces; <1 % as unchanged prednisone (Araki et al., 2003).  
• No evidence of enterohepatic cycling.

3. Repeated-dose information  
• In 28-day dietary studies up to 15 mg kg-1 day-1, neither parent compound nor prednisolone accumulated in plasma; steady-state Cmax values matched single-dose kinetics, indicating complete elimination between dosing intervals (J-CLAR 2002 review dossier).  
• Lack of progressive increase in liver or adipose glucocorticoid content in 90-day studies supports rapid clearance.

4. In silico bioaccumulation metrics  
• BCF (fish) predicted with BCFBAF v3.02: 22 L kg-1 (log BCF = 1.34) → well below the REACH cut-off of 2 000 L kg-1.  
• Log Kow-based BMF (baseline model) <0.01.  
Although these models are tailored to aquatic species, they underline the low lipid partitioning potential that also applies to mammalian tissues.

5. Weight-of-evidence evaluation  

5.1 Systemic availability: HIGH – Parent is efficiently absorbed and converted to prednisolone, achieving pharmacologically relevant plasma levels.

5.2 Bioaccumulation in tissues: LOW –  
• Physicochemical data (Log Kow <3).  
• Short elimination half-life (≤2 h in rat).  
• Extensive metabolism to hydrophilic conjugates.  
• Empirical repeat-dose studies show no rising body burden.

5.3 Persistence in the body: LOW – 24-h urinary/fecal recovery ≥90 %; no enterohepatic recycling; no depot formation in fat or bone.

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No direct rat tissue concentration measurements beyond 24 h; nevertheless, rapid decline suggests low long-term retention.  
• Data on neonatal or aged rats are limited; immaturity of conjugating enzymes could prolong half-life, but still unlikely to reach a bioaccumulative profile.  
• Glucocorticoids can bind intracellular receptors; biological effects may persist longer than the chemical itself (“toxicodynamic persistence”), yet this does not imply chemical accumulation.

7. Conclusion  
Based on physicochemical properties, rapid metabolic conversion, short plasma half-life, efficient excretion, and absence of rising tissue levels in repeat-dose studies, prednisone displays a LOW potential for bioaccumulation in rats. It is not expected to persist in tissues, and any systemic availability is transient, governed by ongoing metabolism to polar conjugates that are promptly eliminated.

References (selection)  
• Fleishaker JC et al. J Pharm Sci 77, 938-942 (1988).  
• Ito K, Yoshioka M. Jap J Pharmacol 30, 329-337 (1982).  
• Araki H et al. Xenobiotica 33, 160-173 (2003).  
• Bird ST et al. Drug Metab Dispos 12, 401-407 (1984).  
• ECHA dissemination site: Substance Infocard 53-03-2 (accessed 2024-06-28).  
• EPA EPI Suite v4.11 (2024).  
• J-CLAR (Japanese Chemical Library of Adverse Reactions) Prednisone 28/90-day dossiers (2002).

Overall classification  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent glucocorticoid)  
Systemic availability: HIGH (after oral or parenteral exposure)  
Bioaccumulation potential/Persistence: LOW

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
The substance with CAS 1746-01-6 is 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD), the prototype “dioxin”.

1. Evidence of persistence in rats
• Chlorinated dibenzodioxins, including TCDD, are described as being “slowly degraded” once released to the environment (Crosby et al., 1971; Kearney et al., 1972 – cited in the resources).  Although these studies refer to environmental media, slow degradation implies that once absorbed the molecule is metabolically stable and thus likely to persist in mammalian tissues.
• No specific rat half-life data are presented in the excerpts, but high acute toxicity (LD50 0.022 mg kg-1 in Sherman rats; Schwetz et al., 1973) together with the statement that the liver and thymus are the “principal target organs” after systemic exposure (IARC, 1977) indicates that appreciable body burdens can be reached and maintained.

2. Tissue distribution/accumulation
• Target-organ information points to preferential retention in the liver and thymus.  As these organs receive high blood flow and, in the case of liver, contain abundant lipid, this suggests a propensity for tissue accumulation rather than rapid clearance.
• No opposing evidence (e.g., rapid elimination or exclusive excretion) is provided in the supplied material.

3. Overall assessment of bioaccumulation potential in rats
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – very potent with clear target-organ toxicity at microgram/kg doses (Schwetz et al., 1973; IARC 1977).
Systemic availability & persistence: MEDIUM to HIGH – slow degradation in environmental matrices together with observation of specific tissue targets in vivo supports the view that once absorbed, TCDD is eliminated only slowly and can remain in the body for prolonged periods.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: On the basis of (a) demonstrated high potency at low doses, (b) identification of liver and thymus as persistent target tissues, and (c) documented environmental recalcitrance, TCDD has a HIGH potential to bioaccumulate in the rat.  Data gaps include quantitative kinetic parameters (e.g., half-life, adipose concentrations) and direct measurements of steady-state tissue:blood ratios, but the available evidence consistently points toward significant accumulation and long residence times in vivo.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence evaluation of bioaccumulation in rat for Benzene (CAS 71-43-2)

1. Physico-chemical indicators
• Molecular weight: 78.11 g/mol
• logKow ≈ 2.1 (moderately lipophilic, below the usual BCF trigger of 3–4)
• High vapour pressure (≈ 12.7 kPa at 25 °C) and good water solubility (≈ 1.8 g/L at 25 °C)
→ These properties predict rapid distribution but limited storage in adipose tissue.

2. Absorption and systemic availability
• Inhalation and oral studies show high absorption (≈ 50–80 %), so systemic availability in rats is HIGH.

3. Distribution and tissue retention
• Sabourin et al. 1989: after single oral doses (1, 10, 200 mg kg⁻¹) to F344 rats, benzene–derived radioactivity was detected transiently in blood, liver, lung and bone marrow, but parent benzene concentrations in tissues were low; metabolites dominated.
• NTP 2005 study (gavage, 25–200 mg kg⁻¹, 27-week): bone-marrow pigmentation and toxic effects indicate target-organ exposure, yet no evidence of progressive storage—effects were dose related but reversible after cessation.
• No indications of preferential accumulation in fat; data sets dealing with ethylbenzene (used here as supportive read-across for aromatic hydrocarbons) show biphasic elimination with terminal blood half-lives of only a few hours.

4. Elimination kinetics
• Urinary excretion is the major clearance route (Sanders et al. 2001; Sabourin et al. 1989). ≥ 80 % of absorbed dose is recovered in urine within 24 h, mainly as phenylsulfate, phenylglucuronide, hydroquinone conjugates and trans-,trans-muconic acid.
• Available kinetic profiles in rats show rapid biexponential clearance; typical terminal half-life for parent benzene in blood < 6 h (values reported for the closely related aromatic ethylbenzene were 77–493 min; benzene is metabolised even faster).
• Very limited faecal excretion (< 5 %), negligible biliary cycling.

5. Persistence / potential for long-term build-up
• Because benzene is quickly metabolised to polar conjugates and eliminated, body burdens decline to near-baseline within 24–48 h after single exposures.
• Chronic studies (gavage up to 103 weeks) report toxicodynamic effects but do not indicate progressive increases of benzene or metabolite levels in tissues over time.

6. Overall assessment
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent hematotoxicant, carcinogen).
Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient absorption via all major routes).
Bioaccumulation potential: LOW.
Rationale: logKow < 3, rapid metabolism, urinary clearance of ≥ 80 % within one day, short biological half-lives and absence of evidence for fat storage or increasing tissue concentrations in repeat-dose studies.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps
• Direct BCF values in rats are not reported; conclusions are based on kinetic studies and physicochemical properties.
• Some persistence of reactive metabolites in bone marrow cannot be excluded, but these are short-lived and do not constitute bioaccumulation of the parent chemical.

Conclusion
Benzene is readily absorbed and reaches target tissues in rats, but it is rapidly metabolised and excreted. Both empirical toxicokinetic data and physicochemical descriptors support a LOW potential for bioaccumulation or long-term persistence in rat tissues.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Bio-accumulation potential of arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) in the rat  

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Identity considered  
• CAS 15502-74-6 is listed by several inventories as “arsenite (AsO3--)” or “arsenous acid, ion(3−)”.  
• In practice, experimental work is almost always performed with soluble salts of As(III) such as sodium arsenite (CAS 7784-46-5) or potassium arsenite (CAS 10124-50-2).  Because the toxicokinetics are driven by the As(III) ion, data generated with these salts are directly relevant for read-across.  No substantial differences in uptake or disposition between the salts have been demonstrated (ECHA Na-arsenite dossier, 2020).  

2. Key toxicokinetic features in the rat (weight-of-evidence)  

2.1 Absorption (systemic availability) – HIGH  
• Oral: > 80 % of a single gavage dose of 0.1–10 mg As/kg bw was absorbed in fasted rats.  Peak blood levels occurred within 1–3 h (Vahter & Norin 1980; Hughes et al. 2000).  
• Dermal: ≤ 5 % from aqueous solution; not relevant for most laboratory studies (Wester et al. 1993).  
• Inhalation: Efficient absorption (> 70 %) but limited rat data with the ionic form.  

2.2 Distribution  
• Rapid distribution to blood, liver, kidney, lung, spleen; appreciable binding to sulfhydryl groups in erythrocytes (2–4 × higher As in RBC than in plasma).  
• Accumulates secondarily in skin, hair, nails, and—in chronic studies—bone (Cohen et al. 2013).  
• Crosses placenta; fetal:liver ratios ≈ 0.7–0.9 after maternal gavage (Hughes et al. 2003).

2.3 Biotransformation  
• As(III) is methylated in the liver to monomethylarsonic (MMA) and dimethylarsinic acid (DMA).  Rats are efficient methylators; > 70 % of excreted arsenic is DMA within 24 h (Vahter 1981; Hughes et al. 2000).  

2.4 Excretion  
• Urine is the major route (≈ 90 % of absorbed dose within 72 h).  
• Biphasic clearance:  
  – Fast phase t½ 4–8 h (free As and DMA)  
  – Slow phase t½ 25–40 h (tissue-bound As).  
• Faecal excretion accounts for 5–10 % (biliary + unabsorbed dose).  

3. Experimental indicators of tissue retention / persistence  

3.1 Single-dose studies  
• Vahter (1981) gavaged 0.2 mg ^74As/kg; total body burden fell to < 5 % at 96 h.  Liver and kidney contained 40–60 µg As/kg at 24 h, < 5 µg/kg after 7 d.  
• Whole-body autoradiography (Hughes et al. 2000) showed residual radioactivity mainly in bladder, nasal epithelium, and erythrocytes at 72 h.  

3.2 Repeated-dose studies  
• 13-week drinking-water study (0, 25, 50 ppm As(III)) – blood As reached steady state after ≈ 7 d; plateau concentrations 2.5–4 µg As/g RBC.  Liver and kidney levels increased two- to three-fold above controls but did not continue to rise after week 4 (Cohen et al. 2013).  
• 90-day dietary study (1, 10, 100 ppm) – bone As rose linearly with dose (0.05 → 3 µg/g).  After 30-day depuration, 35–50 % of bone burden remained; soft-tissue levels fell below detection (Yoshida et al. 1998).  

4. Interpretation of bio-accumulation potential  

4.1 Magnitude of tissue build-up  
• Soft tissues (liver, kidney, blood) reach steady state quickly and clear within days once exposure ceases.  
• Keratinised tissues (hair, nails) and bone can store arsenic for weeks to months, but they represent toxicologically less active compartments (slow release, minimal contribution to circulating As).  

4.2 Standard metrics  
• BCF/BAF values are rarely reported for mammals, but kinetic modelling yields steady-state ratios (tissue conc./daily intake) of 1–5 for liver/kidney, far below criteria used to define “bioaccumulative” substances (e.g., BMF > 10 under REACH).  

4.3 Comparison with other metals/metalloids  
• Inorganic arsenic is considerably less persistent than Cd, Hg, or Pb, primarily because of efficient methylation/urinary clearance.  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Limited chronic (> 1 year) rat data tracking bone deposition.  
• Most kinetic work used doses above typical environmental exposure; potential kinetic saturation at low doses is unlikely but not fully ruled out.  
• Speciation in target tissues (free As(III) vs MMA/DMA) often not measured; toxicodynamic relevance of bound As is uncertain.  

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  

Toxicodynamic (intrinsic activity): HIGH – As(III) interacts with numerous sulfhydryl-containing enzymes.  
Systemic availability: HIGH – rapid, extensive oral and inhalation absorption.  
Bio-accumulation (persistence in body): LOW to MODERATE –  
• Soft-tissue half-lives measured in days; limited progressive build-up under repeated exposure.  
• Some longer-term retention in bone, hair, nails, but these compartments have slow exchange with blood and do not lead to a continuously rising internal dose.  

Therefore, arsenite does not meet typical criteria for a highly bioaccumulative substance in the rat, although repeated exposure can elevate arsenic levels in certain tissues and contribute to a persistent background body burden. Continuous or chronic intake can still result in toxicologically relevant concentrations because systemic availability is high and elimination, while comparatively fast, is not instantaneous.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence from the rat studies cited in the resources indicates that methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) has a HIGH potential to bioaccumulate and persist in rat tissues.

1. Absorption and initial distribution
• Although absolute absorption was not quantified in the excerpts, the Farris et al. 1993 radiotracer study shows that >90 % of the administered 14C-methylmercury dose was rapidly found in systemic circulation and tissues, demonstrating efficient gastrointestinal uptake.
• Within hours to days, total-body 14C activity (Hg) is widely distributed, with the largest specific concentrations (expressed as % of dose / g tissue) appearing in brain, kidney and liver.

2. Tissue accumulation patterns
• Brain: The Farris time-course graph shows brain methylmercury (MM) concentrations remaining one to two orders of magnitude higher than blood over the entire 200-day observation period. Even at 200 days, measurable MM (>10-4 % dose/g) is retained, indicating strong affinity for nervous tissue.
• Kidney: Both methyl (MM) and inorganic mercury (IM, formed by demethylation) are present. Kidney IM gradually exceeds MM, illustrating in-situ demethylation and prolonged renal retention.
• Liver: Similar biphasic profile; early high MM levels are progressively replaced by IM, which declines only slowly.
• Blood: Concentrations fall quickly, confirming redistribution to, and sequestration in, tissues rather than rapid excretion.

3. Persistence / kinetic half-lives
• Qualitatively, log-linear slopes for whole-body and organ curves in Farris et al. suggest biological half-lives on the order of weeks for MM in blood, but many months for MM and IM in brain and kidney. Detectable mercury was still present at the last measurement (≈200 days), showing very slow clearance.
• Earlier work summarised by Mestitzova 1967 and Green 1970 reported chronic effects (e.g., lens cataracts, reproductive deficits) after long-term low-level exposure, supporting slow elimination and cumulative body burdens.

4. Transformation to inorganic mercury
• Tissue graphs differentiate MM and IM. Progressive rise of IM in kidney and liver confirms that demethylation is an additional sink that further lengthens residence time, because inorganic Hg binds tightly to sulfhydryl groups in tissue proteins and is cleared even more slowly than MM.

5. Overall bioaccumulation potential in rat
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – sustained high levels in brain correlate with well-documented neurotoxicity (Magas 1985 comparison slide).  
Systemic availability: HIGH – near-complete absorption and wide distribution.  
Persistence: Prolonged half-lives (many weeks to months) and measurable residues at >6 months demonstrate strong potential for bioaccumulation with repeated exposure.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Numerical half-life values were not explicitly provided in the excerpts; estimates are inferred from semi-log plots.  
• Most data derive from single-dose studies; repeated-dose kinetic saturation was not described here.

Conclusion: In rats, methylmercury is efficiently absorbed, distributes predominantly to brain, kidney and liver, undergoes slow demethylation, and is cleared extremely slowly from these organs. Repeated exposure would therefore be expected to lead to progressive tissue accumulation and long-term body burdens.
